metadata.seed: Yeah, I mean, when I can't soothe her and I just hand her off, I feel like I'm not a good mom to her. Like, I was with George.

patient.query: Yeah, I mean, when I can't soothe her and I just hand her off, I feel like I'm not a good mom to her. Like, I was with George.

automatic_thoughts: I'm not a good mom

entailment: Worried about being a bad mother (finding)
contradiction: Worried about being a bad father (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling really disappointed in yourself when you can’t soothe your baby and have to hand her off. That kind of self‑judgment can be tough to carry around. It’s understandable that you’re worried about being a good mom.

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: worry about being a bad mother
retrieved_concepts_used.label: unknown

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I do feel disappointed. It's just... I keep thinking that I should be able to handle it better, you know? When I can't calm her down, it feels like I'm failing somehow, like I'm not the mom I should be. It’s exhausting.

automatic_thoughts: I should be able to handle it better
I'm failing somehow
I'm not the mom I should be

entailment: Worried about being a bad mother (finding)
contradiction: Worried about being a bad father (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling really let down and exhausted, and you’re worried you’re not meeting the expectations you have for yourself as a mom. That can feel overwhelming when you’re trying to calm her down.

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: worry about being a bad mother
retrieved_concepts_used.label: unknown

patient.query: Yeah, exactly. I just want her to be happy and feel safe, but it feels like I'm never doing it right. Sometimes I just feel like I’m walking on eggshells, and it makes me second-guess everything I do. It’s like I’m not good enough for her or for anyone, really.

automatic_thoughts: I'm never doing it right
I'm not good enough for her
I'm not good enough for anyone

neutral: Worried about being a bad mother (finding)
Worried about being a bad father (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure to be perfect for her. What evidence do you have that you’re never doing it right, and can you recall any moments where you felt you did something that made her smile? A supportive friend might see those moments as proof that you’re doing a good job, even if it doesn’t feel like it right now.

patient.query: I mean, there are moments, sure. Like when I read her bedtime stories and she snuggles up close, or when we bake cookies together and she’s laughing. But then something goes wrong, and it just overshadows everything else. It’s hard to hold onto those good moments when I feel like I'm failing as soon as things get tough. I guess I just fixate on the mistakes.

automatic_thoughts: I'm failing when things get tough
I fixate on the mistakes

neutral: Worried about being a bad mother (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel you fail as soon as things get tough. What evidence do you have that you are failing? Are there moments when you handled things well or learned something from a mistake? How might you reframe those moments to see them as part of learning rather than failure?

patient.query: It’s… it’s a tricky thought process for me. I mean, I just feel like the mistakes are so loud, you know? When I think about handling moments well, they don’t always stick in my mind like the failures do. I guess there have been times when I learned something, but it often just feels like a tiny drop in an ocean of what I could have done better. I don’t know if I can really see it as learning. It just feels like I’m still

automatic_thoughts: mistakes are so loud
failures stick in my mind
tiny drop in an ocean of what I could have done better
I can't really see it as learning

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Forgets what was going to do (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of weight from past mistakes. What evidence do you have that these failures are louder than your successes? Maybe we can look at some moments where you did handle things well and see how they compare.

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: tiny drop in an ocean of what I could have done better
retrieved_concepts_used.label: automatic_thoughts

patient.query: I guess I just remember the really tough moments more vividly. Like, when I lost my temper or couldn't figure out how to comfort her. Those stick with me and replay in my head. It’s like, even if I did something right, I still feel this nagging sense that it wasn’t enough. It’s hard to shake off that feeling, and I wonder if I’ll ever stop thinking about the times I messed up.

automatic_thoughts: I did something right but it wasn't enough
I'll never stop thinking about my mistakes

llm_response.response: It sounds like those moments feel especially heavy for you. What evidence do you have that the things you did were actually enough, and what evidence might suggest otherwise? How might a supportive friend view the same situation?

patient.query: Yeah, they do feel really heavy. I mean, I can think of a few people telling me I'm doing a good job, but it’s hard to hold onto that when I get stuck focusing on my mistakes. Like, maybe I get a compliment, and I think, “Well, they don’t see the full picture.” A friend? I guess they'd probably remind me about the good stuff, like the fun we had baking or how she smiles when I read to her. But I can’t

triggers: people telling me I'm doing a good job
friend reminding me about good stuff
automatic_thoughts: they don’t see the full picture
I focus on my mistakes

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response:
It sounds like the compliments feel fleeting because you keep turning back to the mistakes you see in yourself.  
What evidence do you have that the people who praise you are overlooking your strengths, and can you think of moments when they’ve actually recognized them?  
Could you imagine a way of viewing those compliments that acknowledges both the good and the room for growth, rather than seeing them as incomplete?

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel like I’m just spinning back to the negative. I guess I just think that people only see what they want to see or maybe they’re being nice. Like… I remember a couple of friends said I was a good mom after a fun day with my daughter. But I just keep asking myself, "Did they see the way I lost it when she wouldn't stop crying?" It’s almost like those moments overshadow everything else, and I don’t know how to blend the good

automatic_thoughts: people only see what they want to see
they're being nice
did they see the way I lost it

neutral: Worried about being a bad mother (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing a pattern where a single moment feels like it defines the whole day. What evidence do you have that the rest of the day was also negative? How might you view the compliments you received in light of that? What might a supportive friend say about the balance of good and challenging moments?

patient.query: I guess… I don’t really sit down and think about the rest of the day in that way. I just fixate on the tough spots because they stand out more to me. Like, I could have a great day, but then one meltdown feels like it wipes everything clean. A friend might say that it’s normal to have ups and downs, and that those good moments matter, too. But it’s hard to take that in when I feel like I’m always coming up short.

automatic_thoughts: I always come up short
the tough spots stand out more
one meltdown wipes everything clean

neutral: Able to remember day of the week (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like those tough moments feel like a big weight, making it hard to see the good parts of your day. You mentioned feeling like you’re always coming up short—what would it look like if you could notice one small positive thing that day?

patient.query: I guess if I could notice just one small positive thing, maybe it would be a moment when we were laughing together, like when she made a funny face while trying to help me bake. But even thinking about it, I worry I’d just brush it off as not being a big deal. It feels like I’m always searching for something bigger or more impactful to really count. I don’t know, it just feels… complicated.

automatic_thoughts: worry I’d brush it off
searching for something bigger
feels complicated

neutral: Character trait finding of emotional warmth (finding)

llm_response.response: What evidence do you have that a brief laugh together isn’t a meaningful positive moment? Could it be that noticing even a small joy might shift how you feel about the day? How might you feel if you let that moment stay in your mind a little longer?
